title: Workbook — Sacramentals and Funerals
html:
Student Workbook

For use after class or in a group. Print this page or work in your notebook.

Remembering the Essentials

  1. What is a sacramental? How does it differ from a sacrament?
  2. Name the three main types of sacramentals.
  3. What does it mean that sacramentals work ex opere operantis Ecclesiae?
  4. What are the three parts of Christian funerals?
  5. What is the Church's teaching on cremation?

For Reflection and Writing

Answer each question honestly.

1. What sacramentals do you use in your daily life? Do you use them with faith or out of habit?

Holy water, crucifix, rosary, scapular, medal, image of Mary, a Bible in the home... Make a list. What do they really mean to you? Do they help you draw closer to God or are they just decorative objects?

 

 

2. Are you afraid of death? Where does that fear come from? What helps you face it with Christian hope?

St. Paul says: "For to me, living is Christ and dying is gain" (Phil 1:21). Can you say that honestly? What would need to change for you to be able to say it?

 

 

 

3. Have you ever attended a Catholic funeral? What struck you most?

The funeral Mass, the Paschal candle beside the coffin, the prayers of farewell, the incense rising like our prayers. Did you understand what was happening or was it just a ceremony that "had to be done"?

 

 

 

4. Does your culture have traditions for honoring the dead? How do you live them from faith and not just as folklore?

Whether it is Day of the Dead, All Souls' Day, visiting cemeteries, or memorial services —are these moments of prayer for you or just family gatherings? What could you add to make them express your Catholic faith more fully?

 

 

 

 

5. Who taught you to make the sign of the cross, to pray the rosary, to use holy water? Have you thanked them?

It was probably your mother, your grandmother, a catechist. Those traditions of piety they passed on to you are a treasure. Have you told them? Are you passing them on to someone else?

 

 

 

Practical Activities

A. Bless your home as on Epiphany

Get blessed chalk (ask for it at your parish near January 6) or use holy water. Write above your main doorframe: 20 + C + M + B + [current year]. Pray an Our Father and ask Christ to bless your home. Explain the meaning to your family.

 

 

 

B. Pray a rosary for the deceased in your family

Dedicate a full rosary —five mysteries— to the deceased in your family. Before starting, name them one by one: "For the eternal rest of..." If possible, pray before a photo or memento. Unite your prayer to that of the whole Church, which intercedes for the faithful departed.

 

 

 

 

C. Visit a cemetery and pray for those who have no one to pray for them

Go to your local cemetery. Walk among the graves. Look for the most abandoned ones, those without flowers or candles. Pray an Our Father and a Hail Mary for those souls. It is a spiritual work of mercy: to pray for the living and the dead.

 

 

 

 

D. Do your own "sacramentals audit"

Walk through your home. Is there a visible crucifix? Do you keep a Bible in a worthy place? Do you have holy water? Are there sacred images or just worldly decoration? Make a list of three things you can do to make your home "speak" of your faith to anyone who enters. This isn't about turning your home into a religious museum but about giving the sacred its proper place.

 

 

 

 

Verse to Memorize

"The life of those who believe in you, Lord, is not ended but transformed; and when our earthly dwelling turns to dust, an eternal dwelling is prepared for us in heaven."

— Preface for the Dead I

Prayer for This Week

Lord Jesus, conqueror of death,
you who wept at the tomb of your friend Lazarus
and then called him forth alive from the grave:
teach me to look at death through your eyes.
Help me not to live as if this world were final,
but as a pilgrim journeying toward your home.
May the sacramentals I use each day
—holy water, the sign of the cross, the rosary—
remind me that I belong to you
and that nothing, not even death itself, can separate me from your love.
Grant, Lord, eternal rest to my loved ones
who have departed from this world,
and let perpetual light shine upon them.
Amen.

Print Workbook
